results.possibleIdentity: Alfons Schilling (May 20, 1934 – June 19, 2013) was a Swiss artist known for his pioneering contributions to action painting, spin art, and the technological exploration of visual perception. Active primarily in Vienna and New York, Schilling's career began in the late 1950s at the Academy of Applied Arts in Vienna, where he was closely associated with the Viennese Actionism movement and collaborated with artist Günter Brus. In the early 1960s, he developed 'rotation paintings' (spin art) using motorized circular surfaces to explore movement and space. After moving to New York in 1962, he became an associate of Billy Klüver and was active in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle, famously filming the landmark 1966 event '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering.' His work in the 1970s and 1980s focused on binocular vision, stereoscopy, and holography, leading to the invention of 'vision machines' and virtual reality helmets that challenged conventional sensory experiences. He received a Guggenheim Fellowship in 1985 and served as a professor at the University of Applied Arts in Vienna from 1986 to 1990.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American conceptual and environmental artist who became a leading figure in the late 1960s and 1970s for her interdisciplinary work spanning science, philosophy, mathematics, and technology. She is widely recognized as a pioneer of ecological art (Eco-Logic) and land art. Denes was an active participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from 1969 and 1970 show her involvement in the group, including her submission of proposals for E.A.T.'s "Projects Outside Art." Her early career was marked by the landmark ecological performance piece "Rice/Tree/Burial" (1968) and her "Philosophical Drawings" series (begun in 1969), which utilized complex logic and mathematical systems. In 1970, she was featured in the influential exhibition "Software" at the Jewish Museum in New York, which explored the intersection of art and information technology. She remains best known for her 1982 work "Wheatfield – A Confrontation," where she planted two acres of wheat on a landfill in lower Manhattan near the World Trade Center.

results.possibleIdentity: Vicki Hodgetts is an American artist and graphic designer active in the late 1960s and 1970s, best known for her contributions to feminist art and her association with the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts). She was a prominent participant in the Feminist Art Program and a key collaborator in the landmark 1972 exhibition 'Womanhouse' in Los Angeles. In 'Womanhouse,' Hodgetts co-created the 'Nurturant Kitchen' (with Susan Frazier and Robin Weltsch), which featured her famous 'Eggs to Breasts' installation—a mixed-media work where hundreds of foam or plastic fried eggs on the ceiling transitioned into breast forms on the walls to critique domestic roles and maternal nurturance. Beyond installation art, Hodgetts worked as a graphic designer and is credited with the design of album covers in the early 1970s, such as the 1973 self-titled LP for the band Sand. She was closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle through her collaboration and relationship with architect Craig Hodgetts, who was a lead designer for the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. Her work is frequently cited in histories of the Los Angeles feminist art movement and the early experimental years of CalArts.

results.possibleIdentity: Steven Reiner is an Emmy Award-winning journalist, television producer, and educator who has been active in media and video-related fields since the late 1960s. He gained early prominence as the editor-in-chief of The Daily Cardinal, the student newspaper at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, during the politically active years of 1969 and 1970. Reiner transitioned into a distinguished career in broadcast journalism, serving for more than a decade as a producer for the CBS News program '60 Minutes,' where he frequently collaborated with correspondent Morley Safer. His professional credits also include serving as the executive producer of National Public Radio's (NPR) 'All Things Considered' and working as a producer and writer for ABC News, NBC News, and The Atlantic. In the early 1970s, he was involved in documenting countercultural movements, such as the commune movement, contributing to publications like 'The First Issue.' Reiner has maintained a connection to the history of video and media through his role as an associate professor and Director of Video Journalism at Stony Brook University. He has been associated with retrospectives of the 1960s and 1970s media scenes, moderating panels that include video art pioneers such as Ira Schneider (a member of the Raindance video collective). While he is primarily recognized for his work in journalism and video production, his involvement in the early video journalism movement and his association with figures from the New York and Madison media scenes overlap with the timeframe and mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

wikidata_candidates.label: Jane Holzer
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The name matches exactly, and the Wikidata entity describes the well-known 'Warhol Superstar' Jane Holzer. Her activities as a model, actor, and film producer in the late 1960s align perfectly with the card date (1969) and the provided artistic context.

results.possibleIdentity: Jane Holzer (born Jane Brukenfeld on October 23, 1940), also known as "Baby Jane" Holzer, is an American art collector, film producer, model, and actress who became one of the most prominent figures of the 1960s New York art scene. She is famously recognized as the first "Warhol superstar," having been a close confidante and collaborator of Andy Warhol. She starred in several of his early experimental films, including Soap Opera (1964), Couch (1964), Kiss (1964), and Camp (1965), and was the subject of numerous Screen Tests. Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) stems from the late 1960s through her husband, real estate developer Leonard Holzer. In 1969, Leonard Holzer commissioned E.A.T. to curate and install a series of site-specific artworks at the Smith Haven Mall in Long Island; Jane was considered a driving force behind this project, and the iconic Alexander Calder sculpture installed at the mall, titled 'Janey Waney' (1969), was named in her honor. Beyond her performance work with Warhol, she has had a long career as a major collector of contemporary art (notably works by Basquiat and Keith Haring) and as a producer for films such as Kiss of the Spider Woman (1985) and Spring Breakers (2012).

results.possibleIdentity: Jack (Jacob) Perlmutter (1920–2006) was an American painter and printmaker active primarily from the 1940s through the 1980s. Born in New York City, he later moved to Washington, D.C., where he became a prominent figure in the local art scene and served as the Chairman of the Graphics Department at the Corcoran Gallery School of Art from 1960 to 1982. Perlmutter is notably recognized for his involvement in the NASA Art Program during the late 1960s and 1970s, where he was commissioned to document space exploration, including the Apollo 6 and Apollo 16 missions. His work for NASA, such as the woodblock print 'One Giant Step' (1969) and the oil painting 'Lift-off at 15 Seconds' (1982), is held in the collections of the Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum. His association with technological subjects and his activity during the peak years of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement align with the description of an artist exploring the intersection of art and science in that era.

Jacob Perlmutter is a contemporary British filmmaker, street photographer, and musician based in London. While his own career began in the late 2000s (graduating from film school in 2011), he is frequently associated with historical artists from the 1960s and 1970s through his documentary work. He and his partner, Manon Ouimet, directed the 2024 award-winning feature documentary 'Two Strangers Trying Not to Kill Each Other,' which profiles the relationship between octogenarian street photographer Joel Meyerowitz—a pioneer of color photography in the 1960s—and artist/writer Maggie Barrett. Perlmutter has also served as a photographer documenting art-historical conferences and exhibitions, such as the 'Axis of Solidarity' symposium at Tate Modern, which examines transnational art movements of the 1960s through 1980s.

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Newman is a British cartoonist and comic book illustrator who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Newman obtained his first professional role in 1966 as a creative director and consultant. He spent approximately 35 years illustrating comics and working in the advertising industry. In his later years, he has been known to share his skills through comic drawing workshops in Slough and Henley-on-Thames.

Martin Newman was a theatrical stage manager active in the New York art and performance scene during the mid-1960s. He is credited as the stage manager for the original Broadway production of 'Man of La Mancha' in 1965. Martin Newman was an executive in the motion picture and technology field in New York during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Serving as the Vice President and Treasurer of Century Theatres, he was a key figure in the National Association of Theatre Owners (NATO). In 1969, he led the Pay-TV Committee, which engaged with emerging broadcast and cable technologies. results.possibleIdentity: Paul Fromm (1906–1987) was a prominent German-born American wine merchant and one of the most significant patrons of contemporary classical music in the United States. He founded the Fromm Music Foundation (FMF) in 1952, which became a vital force in the avant-garde music scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. Active in supporting experimental and technological intersections in music, he moved his foundation to Harvard University in 1972. Fromm was instrumental in establishing the influential journal 'Perspectives of New Music' (1962) and organized the 'Fromm Weeks' at Tanglewood, which focused on contemporary compositions. His foundation's activities were closely linked with the burgeoning field of computer music and electronic synthesis, including support for and correspondence with pioneers like John Chowning and Milton Babbitt. This technological focus aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that promoted collaborations between artists and engineers; historical accounts of E.A.T. and computer music archives frequently reference Fromm's patronage as a key infrastructural support for these interdisciplinary works.

Paul Fromm was a specialized engineer active in the late 1960s who contributed to the Apollo space program, including work on NASA's lunar training vehicle. results.possibleIdentity: Tim Young is a prominent British mastering and cutting engineer whose career began in 1971 at Trident Studios in London. He is highly regarded in the music industry for his technical and creative work on seminal albums, most notably mastering 'London Calling' (1979) by The Clash. Young's career spans several decades, including a significant tenure at CBS Studios (later Whitfield Street Recording Studios) and his co-founding of Metropolis Mastering in 1993. He was the first non-US mastering engineer to win a Grammy Award, which he received for his work on the 5.1 surround sound mastering of The Beatles' 'Love' album. His professional identity as a technical expert who collaborates closely with artists to shape their final sound aligns with the core collaborative goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which paired artists with engineers to produce innovative works.

Timothy Young is a writer, illustrator, animator, and designer who has had a multi-decade career in the visual and applied arts. While his most recognized work as an author and illustrator of children's books (such as 'I Hate Picture Books!' and 'The Angry Little Puffin') gained prominence later, his early career included roles as an animation model maker and toy designer. He contributed to the first season of 'Pee-wee's Playhouse' in the mid-1980s and designed characters and toys for Jim Henson Productions, Disney, and Universal Studios. Some historical retrospectives on computer graphics also credit a Timothy Young with providing concept sketches for early technical animation projects, such as 'Waldo C. Graphic' (1988), in contexts that often reference the broader influence of technology-focused art initiatives like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth J. Dunkley (born December 29, 1939) is an American physicist, inventor, and holographic artist known for his pioneering work at the intersection of science and visual art. Active since the late 1960s, he is highly regarded in the field of holography for creating "Thoughts" (1973), which is recognized as one of the first art holograms to fully utilize the holographic medium through independent "serially connected" visual spaces. This work is considered a pivotal example of holography's development as an art form. Dunkley’s artistic and scientific pursuits align with the collaborative mission of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as he applied his background in physics and human vision research to create immersive visual experiences. His holographic art was featured in the 1977 "Holography" exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York. Professionally, he is the inventor of Three-Dimensional Viewing Glasses (3-DVG), patented in 1986, which allow viewers to see 2D images in 3D without lenses by blocking specific points in peripheral vision. He is the president of Holospace Laboratories Inc. and has conducted visual effects workshops at the Museum of Scientific Discovery in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.

results.possibleIdentity: Gabe Fielding (also known as Gabriel Fielding) is an art director, designer, and photographer who was active in the rock and roll touring and production industry starting in the late 1960s or early 1970s. He is specifically noted for his long-term collaboration with the band The Who, for whom he provided art direction, programme design, and photography. He is credited for the design, compilation, and art direction of the tour program for The Who's rock opera "Tommy" (notably for their 1989 reunion tour, though his association with the band's technical and creative circles dates back much earlier). Fielding was part of a close-knit group of technical and creative professionals often referred to as the "rock and roll fraternity," which included renowned front-of-house engineer Gordon "Gungi" Paterson and pyrotechnician Peter "Pyro Pete" Kappeler. He was associated with ML Executives, the production and rental company founded by members and associates of The Who in the early 1970s. His work frequently intersected the worlds of art and technology, consistent with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted collaborations between artists and engineers for large-scale technical spectacles such as those pioneered by The Who.

results.possibleIdentity: Marti Cohen (later known as Marti Cohen-Wolf) was a central administrative figure and coordinator for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization established in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she served as an administrative assistant and operations coordinator under E.A.T.'s leadership, which included Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. Cohen was instrumental in managing the logistics for major E.A.T. projects, most notably the 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum and the ambitious Pepsi Pavilion project for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. She was also listed as a contributor and editor for E.A.T. publications, including the newsletter Techne. In later decades, she remained active in the New York City art and civic community, serving as a public member of Community Board 1 and advocating for local cultural and library projects in Battery Park City.

results.possibleIdentity: Kris A. Dines (born November 12, 1949; died September 19, 2017) was an electrical engineer and researcher who was active in the early 1970s, particularly known for his work in high-technology imaging fields such as acoustical holography and computerized tomography. While primarily an engineer, his work in acoustical holography during his time at Purdue University (Class of 1972) aligned closely with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which fostered collaborations between engineers and artists on cutting-edge technologies like holography and lasers. Dines co-authored foundational research on computerized tomographic imaging with Avinash C. Kak and Malcolm Slaney, and his early 1970s research at Purdue involved three-dimensional reconstruction from acoustic holograms. Many technical participants in E.A.T. projects from this era were engineers from institutions like Purdue and Bell Labs who collaborated with artists on immersive and experimental installations.

Allen Dines (also referred to as A. Dines) was an engineer and technical associate of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during its peak activity in the late 1960s and early 1970s. E.A.T. records from 1968 list him as a technical member, and he was involved in promoting the organization's goal of interdisciplinary collaboration. A more modern individual of the same name, active at the University of Wisconsin-Madison as an entrepreneurship advocate and 'Venture Designer,' describes himself as an 'Artist, Engineer, and Programmer' and has been involved in technology transfer and innovative community projects like the '100-Hour Challenge' and 'Hybrid Zone X.' This multidisciplinary profile reflects the historical spirit of the E.A.T. movement which sought to dissolve the boundaries between art, science, and engineering.
